summary:
The Taosi site in Shanxi province, China, occupied approximately 2300–2000 BC during the late Longshan period, is one of the most significant prehistoric archaeological sites in China and a strong candidate for identification with the early phases of Chinese proto-state formation. The site covers an area of approximately 2.8 square kilometres and shows evidence of clear social stratification, large-scale planning, and institutional complexity characteristic of early state societies.

Excavations at Taosi have revealed a walled enclosure interpreted as a palace-administrative district, large public storage areas, workshops for luxury craft production, and an astronomical observatory — a curved rammed-earth structure with calibrated slots through which sunrise observations could be made at different times of the year. This solar observatory, if correctly interpreted, represents one of the earliest known purpose-built astronomical instruments in the world and indicates a sophisticated interest in calendar regulation linked to agricultural management and ritual.

The material culture of Taosi includes richly furnished elite burials with jade objects, lacquerware, painted pottery, and musical instruments — a range of prestige goods suggesting a ruling class that maintained its position through control of ritual, craft production, and agricultural surplus. The site also shows evidence of violent conflict: a phase of destruction, including skeletal remains showing evidence of violence and dismemberment, suggests that Taosi experienced political upheaval around 2000 BC. Taosi is frequently discussed in the context of the [[event:1769|legendary Xia dynasty]] and as evidence for the emergence of early Chinese state formation.
